payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
"Provenance" has become a crowded word in music infrastructure. Detection vendors use it. Stem-matching products use it. Watermarking systems use it. Each product solves a real problem, but they're not the same problem, and treating them as one provenance category obscures what the work actually requires.

There's a clean line that separates real provenance from the products borrowing the language. Falsifiability.

Cryptographic signatures cannot be falsified in the technical sense. Detection scores can be. Stem-matching results can be. The distinction sounds academic until you put it in front of a royalty dispute, at which point it becomes the entire point.

What unfalsifiable means

Cryptographic signatures are unfalsifiable in a specific technical sense. Either the signature verifies against the public key, or it doesn't. There's no probability score. No confidence interval. No edge case where the math is "almost right." A valid signature is proof that the holder of the private key signed the data. Period.

This is the same primitive that authorizes wire transfers at every bank. The same one that signs every code update an operating system installs. The same one that secures every TLS connection a browser makes. The same one on the chip in a passport. The math has been audited continuously for decades and the failure modes are well understood. When signatures fail, it's because private keys leak, not because the system is uncertain.

Unfalsifiable doesn't mean unhackable. It means that within the boundaries of the system, the claim is binary. If the signature verifies, the signer signed it. If it doesn't, they didn't. There is no third state.

The pushback worth answering

The pushback at this point is that signatures verify keys, not people. True, and beside the point. Identity binding has established solutions across passports, banking, and code-signing certificates. Content-based detection has no solution at the math layer. Provenance moves the dispute from where it cannot be settled to where it can. The remaining work is operational, not epistemic.

What falsifiable means in detection

Detection systems and pattern-matching systems produce falsifiable claims by design. A detector says "this track is 87% likely to be AI-generated." A stem matcher says "this matches a verified human stem with 94% confidence." Both claims include their own uncertainty.

Falsifiable claims are useful for triage. They are not useful for adjudication. In a royalty dispute, "94% confident" is an opening argument, not an answer. The other side argues for the 6%. The case goes to human review, then to legal review, then settles or doesn't. The dispute extends.

This is true even for very accurate detection systems. A detector with 99.9% accuracy is wrong on one of every thousand tracks it evaluates. At any meaningful platform scale, that produces enough false positives to feed continuous disputes. Better models lower the rate. They don't change the category of claim. Probabilistic claims remain probabilistic regardless of how good the probability gets.

Detection versus provenance: probabilistic against binary
Detection produces probabilistic, falsifiable claims that fall somewhere on a continuous zero to one hundred percent scale, with an example reading at eighty-seven percent. Provenance produces binary, unfalsifiable claims with only two possible states, valid or invalid.

DETECTION PRODUCES

0%
100%

87%

Probabilistic. Falsifiable.

PROVENANCE PRODUCES

VALID
INVALID

Binary. Unfalsifiable.

Why music needs the unfalsifiable primitive

Music distribution runs on disputes. Royalty splits between writers, performers, producers, masterers. Mechanical licenses, performance rights, sync rights. Master recording versus composition. Sample clearances. Public domain edge cases. Black box payments where the rights holder cannot be identified. When The MLC opened in 2021, twenty-one DSPs transferred $424 million in historical unmatched royalties that could not be paid because rights holders could not be identified. The metadata layer is structurally broken.

A verification system that ends disputes is worth a different category of money than one that contributes evidence to disputes. Banks pay for unfalsifiable signatures because they end the dispute about whether a transfer was authorized. Operating systems pay for code signing because it ends the dispute about whether an update came from the vendor. Music has the same dispute volume and currently has neither.

Detection products will improve. Accuracy will get better. False positive rates will get lower. None of that changes the fact that the claim is falsifiable, which means it can be challenged. Music does not need claims that can be challenged less often. It needs proofs that cannot be challenged at all.

The line between provenance and everything-else-that-uses-the-word-provenance runs along this distinction. If the claim is statistical, it's evidence. If the claim is mathematical, it's proof. Evidence is useful. Proof is the layer the industry actually needs.

Falsifiability is the line. Triage tools sit on one side. Infrastructure sits on the other.
payload.title: Falsifiability is the line
